text: The surgeon begins the operation by making an incision in the peritoneum on the right side, medial to the right ureter, at the level of the sacral promontory.
The surgeon deepens the incision until reaching the presacral fascia, developing the surgical plane both inferiorly and superiorly.
The surgeon demonstrates the development of the avascular plane between the presacral fascia and the rectum using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques.
The surgeon uses a hook to perform dissection down to the bottom of the sacral halo, carefully navigating the anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs further resection at the lower end, extending beyond the tip of the coccyx until the fibers of the pelvic diaphragm or levator muscles become visible.
The surgeon completes the resection, ensuring that a finger introduced digitally from below is visible through the laparoscopic view, indicating the extent of the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection on both sides of the rectum, maintaining the avascular plane between the presacral fascia and the rectum.
The surgeon uses a hook to divide the right lateral ligament, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection, advancing below until reaching the sacral hollow, a concave area at the base of the spine.
The surgeon completes the dissection by dividing the right lateral attachments of the rectum, following the previous division of the right lateral ligament.
The surgeon performs dissection on the left lateral side of the rectum, completing the separation of the left lateral attachments using surgical instruments.
Following the completion of dissection, the surgeon focuses on the peritoneal reflection of the rectosigmoid colon at the lateral pelvic wall, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum from top to bottom, revealing the underlying structures as part of the rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon performs careful hook dissection layer by layer, deepening the incision until the left ureter is visible and entering the sacral hollow from the right side, ensuring the ureter is safeguarded and the correct plane is maintained.
The surgeon continues the dissection by cutting the lateral ligaments on the left side, mirroring the previous dissection performed on the right side.
The surgeon completes the dissection on both sides, focusing on the rectosigmoid area and lateral pelvic wall, ensuring all anatomical structures are properly exposed and safeguarded.
The surgeon straightens the rectum after completing the dissection and incising the peritoneum, with the rectum now visible in a more aligned position.
The surgeon places traction on the rectum and sutures it to the sacral promontory using 2-0 polypropylene sutures.
The surgeon places a similar suture on the left side, securing the rectum to the sacral promontory using two O polypropylene sutures.
The surgeon takes a suture from the rectum to the pre-sacral fascia at the level of the sacral promontory, securing the rectum in place.
The rectum is visibly straightened after the completion of suturing to the sacral promontory, demonstrating the effectiveness of the traction applied during the procedure.
The laparoscopic suture rectopexy is completed, with the rectum secured to the sacral promontory using two O polypropylene sutures.
